title: ECB Forum Panel (Sintra)
summary: Warsh's first international appearance as Chair (Sintra panel with Lagarde, Bailey, Macklem) kept the June price-stability anchor intact but struck a marginally softer tone. He reaffirmed that 'prices are too high' and that anyone expecting comfort with inflation above 2% 'would be disappointed', yet volunteered that over his first four weeks 'expectations of inflation ... have come down. Inflation risks have come down,' and declined the 'hawkish' label. He leaned into an AI/productivity supply-side story — structural productivity in the high-2% range, potential growth trending up, a capex boom he expects to expand supply — but explicitly WALLED IT OFF from near-term policy: the boom shows up 'first and foremost in demand' (inflationary now), the supply payoff is later and uncertain ('it's up to the central bank to decide whether it's inflationary'), and 'does that optimism convey into policy in the next six or nine months? Still too soon to say' with the timing 'to be taken very seriously.' So it is a structural narrative, not a reaction-function signal. He also refused all forward guidance on July ('a good family fight in four weeks'), would not update the labor read on recent data, and declined to call the energy shock transitory. Net: mostly a REAFFIRMATION of the June stance, with one genuine but narrow marginal softening — inflation expectations and risks 'have come down' — plus a less strident tone. A modest step down in stridency, not a signal about the next move.

key_quotes.quote: expectations of inflation over the first four weeks of this period, they've come down. Inflation risks have come down.
key_quotes.context: The key softening vs June: he presents inflation risk as already receding under his watch.

key_quotes.quote: we've all looked around, and we've seen that prices are too high. And I don't think I'm the only one on this stage that's recommitted to deliver price stability.
key_quotes.context: Price-stability anchor held firm despite the softer tone.

key_quotes.quote: if there were people in household or the business sector and the financial markets who thought that this central bank was going to be comfortable with an inflation objective above 2%, well, I guess they'd be disappointed.
key_quotes.context: Floor under the dovishness: no tolerance for an above-2% objective; a cut needs inflation to actually cede.

key_quotes.quote: their expectation is the supply side of the economy will expand. And if it does, that has huge implications for monetary policy ... structural productivity is in the high 2% range. So potential growth looks like it's trended up.
key_quotes.context: Structural optimism on supply/productivity; but he separately calls it 'too soon' to convey into policy over the next 6-9 months — a medium-term narrative, not a near-term steer.
